summary: Fowler advocates building platforms through an emergent, bottom-up process rather than upfront design. The approach starts by building a well-designed application without attempting to make it generic. When building subsequent applications with overlapping needs, duplication is factored out into a shared 'proto-platform' that stabilizes over multiple iterations. This harvesting approach, while seemingly less efficient than building a foundation platform first, proves more effective in practice.
key_points: Start by building a well-factored application, not a platform
Identify duplication only when building the second application with similar needs
Factor shared code into a common area that becomes the proto-platform
Keep everything in a single codebase for the first few applications before separating
This emergent approach works better than upfront platform design despite appearing less efficient
money_quotes: To build a platform by harvesting, you start by not trying to build a platform, but by building an application.
While you build the application you don't try to develop generic code, but you do work hard to build a well-factored and well designed application.
As you find duplication you factor out into a common area, this common area is the proto-platform.
While this sounds harder and less efficient than FoundationPlatform it seems to work better in practice.
key_insight: Platforms should emerge from the concrete needs of multiple applications rather than be designed speculatively upfront.
